name: The League
summary: Elite matchmaking app for ambitious professionals; acquired by Match Group in 2019 and shut down in 2024 after failing to scale beyond a niche audience.
lesson: An exclusivity-based niche can build buzz but rarely reaches the scale needed to survive inside a portfolio of larger competitors.

name: Happn
summary: Location-based French dating app that let users see people they'd crossed paths with in real life; underwent mass layoffs in 2022 and shrank dramatically.
lesson: A distinctive location mechanic doesn't guarantee retention once bigger apps copy the core idea with more resources.

name: Iris Dating
summary: AI-driven dating app that claimed to learn users' 'type' from swipes; shut down after being exposed for using fabricated/bot profile photos to appear more populated than it was.
lesson: Faking network density to mask a cold-start problem destroys trust faster than slow organic growth would have cost you.

name: LastPass
summary: Popular password manager that suffered a series of breaches in 2022 exposing encrypted vault data, causing a mass exodus of users and lasting reputational damage.
lesson: For a security product, a single serious breach can permanently erode the trust the entire business model depends on.

name: Cambridge Analytica
summary: Political data-analytics firm that harvested Facebook users' personal data without proper consent for psychographic targeting; collapsed and filed for bankruptcy in 2018 amid global scandal.
lesson: Building a business on covertly harvested personal data creates a regulatory and reputational time bomb, not a durable moat.

name: Ashley Madison
summary: Dating site marketed toward extramarital affairs; suffered a catastrophic 2015 hack that exposed over 30 million users' personal data, triggering lawsuits and lasting brand damage.
lesson: A business model built on user secrecy makes a data breach existential rather than merely embarrassing.

name: Jumio
summary: Early identity-verification pioneer that collapsed into insolvency in 2016 after its CEO was fired for expense-account fraud; assets were later acquired out of bankruptcy and relaunched.
lesson: Founder-level financial misconduct can destroy a well-funded, technically sound company almost overnight, regardless of product quality.

name: Peeple
summary: 'Yelp for people' app letting users publicly rate and review other individuals; faced immediate, overwhelming public backlash over privacy and harassment concerns and never gained real traction.
lesson: Applying a review-platform model directly to human beings crosses a trust threshold that most of the public will reject outright.

name: Civic
summary: Blockchain-based identity verification platform that raised $33M in a 2017 token sale; repeatedly pivoted its product but never achieved mainstream identity-verification adoption.
lesson: Token-funded startups can raise large sums without validating real product demand, delaying the reckoning rather than avoiding it.

name: Cupid plc
summary: UK-listed online dating operator whose stock collapsed after revelations that some of its sites used fake profiles and chatbots to entice paying subscribers into conversations.
lesson: Using fake engagement to juice subscription revenue is a fraud risk that can destroy a public company's valuation almost instantly once exposed.

name: Onerep
summary: Data-removal service that suffered a major reputational scandal in 2024 when it was revealed its founder had also created several of the people-search sites the service was meant to scrub users from.
lesson: A privacy service's credibility depends entirely on the integrity of its operators.

name: PlentyOfFish
summary: One of the earliest large-scale free dating sites, built and run profitably by a single founder for over a decade before being acquired by Match Group in 2015 for $575 million.
lesson: A simple, free, ad-supported product run with extreme capital discipline can outcompete better-funded rivals for over a decade.

name: Badoo
summary: Large international social-discovery dating app founded by Andrey Andreev that became the anchor asset of Bumble Inc., which went public in 2021.
lesson: Global-scale network effects built over many years can become the foundation for a much larger public company.

name: Tantan
summary: Chinese swipe-based dating app often described as 'China's Tinder'; acquired by Momo in 2018 for a reported $735-955 million.
lesson: Localizing a proven Western dating mechanic for a major market with different social norms can create a billion-dollar-scale outcome.

name: Okta
summary: Enterprise identity and access management platform that went public in 2017 and became one of the largest independent identity companies in the world.
lesson: Building identity infrastructure for enterprises rather than consumers created a far more durable, high-margin business model.

name: Socure
summary: AI-driven identity verification and fraud prevention company that reached a valuation of roughly $4.5 billion in 2021, serving major banks and government agencies.
lesson: Combining machine learning with large data networks for fraud detection created a defensible enterprise moat as digital fraud surged.

name: 1Password
summary: Password manager that stayed profitable and bootstrapped for over a decade before raising its first outside funding in 2019, later reaching a valuation of roughly $6.8 billion.
lesson: A profitable, product-led company can wait over a decade to raise venture capital, entering from a position of strength rather than need.

name: Proton
summary: Swiss privacy company behind ProtonMail, ProtonVPN, and related tools; grew from a crowdfunded encrypted email project into a large suite of privacy products serving over 100 million accounts.
lesson: Starting with crowdfunding from a mission-driven community can build a loyal user base that scales into a broad, sustainable privacy product suite.

name: DuckDuckGo
summary: Privacy-focused search engine that avoided tracking users, growing into a profitable, ad-supported business without needing large venture rounds.
lesson: A clear, simple privacy promise can be monetized profitably at meaningful scale without user data harvesting.
